title: Chapter 14 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of performance optimization!

questions.question: What does React.memo do?
options: Memoizes the result of a function
Prevents a component from re‑rendering if its props haven't changed
questions.explanation: React.memo is a higher‑order component that memoizes the rendered output based on props.

questions.question: useMemo can be used to memoize both values and functions.
questions.explanation: Yes, but useCallback is the specialized hook for functions.

questions.question: Which hook would you use to prevent an expensive calculation from running on every render?
questions.explanation: useMemo caches the result of a calculation.

questions.question: When passing a function to a memoized child, you should wrap it with:
questions.explanation: useCallback provides a stable function reference.

questions.question: React.memo performs a deep equality check on props by default.
questions.explanation: No, it performs a shallow comparison.

questions.question: What is a common technique to optimize Context performance?
options: Using useMemo for the context value
questions.explanation: Both splitting contexts and memoizing the value help reduce unnecessary re‑renders.

questions.question: _____ is a tool in React DevTools that helps you record and analyze re‑renders.
questions.explanation: The Profiler tab lets you record interactions and see component render times.

questions.question: What does React.lazy do?
options: Lazily loads a component only when it's rendered
questions.explanation: React.lazy enables code splitting at the component level.

questions.question: Suspense is required when using React.lazy to show a fallback while the component loads.
questions.explanation: Yes, you must wrap lazy components in Suspense.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid performance optimization technique?
questions.explanation: useState is for state management, not optimization.

questions.question: The second argument to useMemo and useCallback is the _____ array.
questions.explanation: The dependency array controls when the memoized value is recomputed.

questions.question: What will happen if you use useMemo without a dependency array?
options: It will run on every render (no memoization)
questions.explanation: If no array is provided, a new value is computed on every render.

questions.question: You should wrap every component with React.memo.
questions.explanation: No, only components that render often with the same props and are expensive to render. Memoization itself has a cost.

questions.question: What is the purpose of code splitting?
options: To split code into smaller files for easier maintenance
To reduce the initial bundle size by loading code on demand
To split the state into multiple reducers
To split the DOM into virtual chunks
questions.explanation: Code splitting improves initial load time by splitting the bundle into chunks loaded as needed.

questions.question: When a context value changes, all _____ re‑render.
questions.explanation: All components that use useContext on that context re‑render.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about useCallback?
options: It memoizes the function's return value
It returns a memoized function that only changes if dependencies change
It prevents the function from being called
It's the same as useMemo for objects
questions.explanation: useCallback returns a memoized function reference.

questions.question: React.lazy works with named exports directly.
questions.explanation: No, it only works with default exports. You can create an intermediate module that re‑exports the named export as default.

questions.question: What does the Profiler in React DevTools measure?
options: Render times and which components re‑rendered
questions.explanation: The Profiler records render timings and re‑render reasons.

questions.question: To catch errors in lazy components, you can use an _____ boundary.
questions.explanation: Error boundaries catch errors in their child tree, including lazy loading failures.

questions.question: Why might you use useMemo to stabilize an object passed as a prop?
options: To prevent a memoized child from re‑rendering when the object's content hasn't changed
questions.explanation: Stabilizing the object reference prevents unnecessary re‑renders of memoized children that depend on it.
